message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"When time-compressed at high rates, NORMAL speech remains more intelligible than CLEAR (hyperarticulated) speech — even after equalizing total speech+silence duration — challenging the assumption that clear-speech corpora are the best training target for fast TTS."

**Source:** https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "We found that even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What is this paper about? Does it compare normal speech vs clear (hyperarticulated) speech under time-compression? What does it conclude about which is more intelligible when compressed/sped up? Quote any findings about normal vs clear speech intelligibility when total duration of speech and silence is equalized.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "time-compressed clear speech vs normal speech intelligibility hyperarticulated equalized duration"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Word-level intelligibility of time-compressed speech: Prosodic and segmental factors","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222395525_Word-level_intelligibility_of_time-compressed_speech_Prosodic_and_segmental_factors"},{"title":"Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.07239"},{"title":"Speaking and Hearing Clearly: Talker and Listener Factors in Speaking Style Changes - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2747755/"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Temporal organization of English clear and conversational speech - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2677354/"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Intelligibility and Acoustic Characteristics of Clear and Conversational Speech in Telugu (A South Indian Dravidian Language) - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3102160/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what research has found about time-compressed clear speech versus normal speech intelligibility:

## Key Finding: Normal Speech Advantage

Even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech. This finding held true even when the duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, where compressed normal speech is still more intelligible.

## Compression Methods

Generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates. Researchers explored various approaches to compression, including compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs.

## Clear Speech Characteristics

Research on hyperarticulated clear speech showed that clear speech had effects on temporal characteristics in consonant and vowel intervals, with sentence-in-noise listening tests showing a consistent clear speech intelligibility gain. However, temporal restructuring accompanied changes in speaking style, including insertion of consonant and vowel segments and an increase in the number of prosodic phrases for clear speech.

The research suggests that when compressing speech to very high rates, maintaining the temporal and articulatory characteristics of normal speech actually produces better intelligibility than relying on clear speech characteristics.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Paper Summary

Yes, this paper directly compares normal versus clear (hyperarticulated) speech under time-compression conditions.

## Main Finding

The research concludes that **compressed normal speech remains more intelligible than compressed clear speech**, even when total duration is equalized. As the abstract states:

> "Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs."

## Key Conclusion on Intelligibility

When researchers controlled for overall duration by compressing speech and silence at different rates:

> "compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech"

This was a surprising finding, since clear speech typically enhances intelligibility in normal-speed conditions. The research suggests that the advantages of clear speech's hyperarticulation diminish or disappear when the material is time-compressed, particularly at higher compression rates.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "clear speech intelligibility advantage time compression high rate listeners normal-hearing contradicting"

Links: [{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/"},{"title":"Temporal properties in clear speech perception - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16875238/"},{"title":"Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.07239"},{"title":"Speaking and Hearing Clearly: Talker and Listener Factors in Speaking Style Changes - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2747755/"},{"title":"Evaluating the role of spectral and envelope characteristics in the intelligibility advantage of clear speech - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2806443/"},{"title":"Clear speech promotes speaking rate normalization | JASA Express Letters | AIP Publishing","url":"https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jel/article/3/5/055205/2892549/Clear-speech-promotes-speaking-rate-normalization"},{"title":"The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9557069/"}]

I've found relevant research on clear speech intelligibility, time compression, and normal-hearing listeners. Here are the key findings:

## Clear Speech Advantage at High Speaking Rates

Interestingly, research presents some contradictory findings regarding clear speech performance at high compression rates. Unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed clear speech at moderate rates, but at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast. Additionally, even when the duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than clear speech.

## Clear Speech Intelligibility Benefits

More generally, clear speech is significantly more intelligible (roughly 17 percentage points) than conversational speech for both normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners in a variety of difficult listening situations. Talkers are able to produce clear speech at a variety of speaking rates, as evident in the clear speech intelligibility advantage for both slow and normal/conversational clear speech over conversational speech.

## Time Compression Effects

Processing artifacts in uniform time scaling, particularly time compression, reduced speech intelligibility. Although fast speech can be 100% intelligible in quiet, higher signal-to-noise ratios are needed to perceive speech intelligibly in noise when presented at faster rates.

These findings suggest that while clear speech generally provides intelligibility benefits, its advantage may diminish or even reverse at extremely high speaking rates due to time compression artifacts.

input.description: Read results and conclusions section of paper

content.content:
more than aid, we compress silence Y times more than speech
for both styles (N-NL3 and C-NL3). Y was chosen to be two.

We were interested in finding whether linearly compressed clear
speech was found to be less intelligible at higher rates because
the duration of speech was shorter due to the presence of longer
silence regions. This was however not the case as when we
set the duration of speech and silence to be the same for compressed normal speech and clear speech, we found that compressed normal speech was more intelligible. One possible reason is that clear speech had to be compressed considerably more
than normal speech which could have caused more artefacts due
to larger phase differences at frame boundaries brought upon by
the compression. The WSOLA implementation used here [24]
suggests no more than 4.0x the compression rate and for many
sentences clear speech was compressed more than this. Future
work will involve reducing such artefacts.
The non linear compression method improved results for
both styles. Unfortunately, this was not enough to make compressed clear speech more intelligible than normal speech. This
is an interesting result as the overhead of applying energy detection is quite small and requires no further delay; it can be
done online as opposed to methods where all silence regions are
first completely removed. We would like to quantify what further intelligibility gains more complicated non linear methods
inspired by fast speech acoustics [16, 18] might bring and particularly whether most of the gain they obtain are due to heavy
silence compression.
Another point of discussion is why the type of clear speech
used in this experiment (read clear speech) was less intelligible
after time compression; this may be because it was not recorded
in a communicative spontaneous task, where acoustic changes
are known to be less extreme [25].

6. Conclusions
4.2. Listening experiment
As in the previous experiment, twenty native English speakers
with no self reported hearing impairment transcribed eight sentences for each condition after hearing each sentence only once.
One participant was removed from the results as his/her word
errors were found to be excessively high compared to the others.
4.3. Results
The results are calculated as the percentage of word errors averaged across a listener, in the same way as in the previous experiment. Fig.3 presents the word error in percentage for the 3x
rate.
Similar to what was found in the previous experiment the
word errors for linearly compressed clear speech (C-L) 39.4%
is around twice as high as results with linearly compressed normal speech (N-L) 20.3%. This relation remains the same when
the final duration of speech and silence is the same across styles:
15.3% of N-NL1 and 28.2% of C-NL2.
Not compressing pauses improved results for both styles
significantly as we see NL1 scores are lower than L, but at the
expense of a longer utterance duration. Compressing silence
twice as much as speech also improves results as we see that
for both styles NL3 results are better than L, even though the
utterance duration is the same. The difference was found to be
significant only for the normal style. All types of non linear

In this paper we exploit the use of clear speech to increase the
intelligibility of speech reproduced at extremely high speaking rates. We first evaluate linearly compressed speech of four
styles produced by the same speaker: normal, fast and two types
of clear speech - computer directed and hearing-impaired directed speech (clear h). We found that unprocessed fast speech
was less intelligible than linearly compressed clear h and normal but at the highest speaking rates clear h was worse than fast.
As possibly the only advantage fast speech has over clear in
terms of intelligibility is the shorter silence regions, we exploit
in our second experiments a range of time compression methods that compress speech and silence differently. We found that
even when the duration of speech and silence is kept the same
across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible. Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved
results for both styles at the expense of very little overhead.

content.text:
I now have complete verification. Let me assess the findings against the claim.

**Verification summary:**

1. **Quote accuracy**: The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate. Abstract (lines 23-24): "when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech." Confirmed in conclusions (lines 437-438).

2. **Human listening test**: Confirmed real subjects. "twenty native English speakers with no self reported hearing impairment transcribed eight sentences for each condition" (line ~423). Two experiments. Concrete numbers: at 3x rate, linearly compressed clear speech (C-L) = 39.4% word error vs normal (N-L) = 20.3% — clear speech roughly TWICE the error rate. Even with equalized duration: N-NL1 = 15.3% vs C-NL2 = 28.2%.

3. **The claim is well-supported by the paper.** The paper directly tests and confirms exactly what the claim states.

**However — important caveats the paper itself raises that the claim omits:**

- The authors offer a confound explanation (line ~388-393): clear speech "had to be compressed considerably more than normal speech which could have caused more artefacts" because WSOLA compression exceeded the recommended 4.0x rate for many sentences. So the normal-speech advantage may be partly a **compression-artifact effect**, not an inherent property of the speech style. The authors flag "Future work will involve reducing such artefacts."
- Line ~408: the clear speech used was **read clear speech**, which they note "may be" less robust than spontaneous communicative clear speech "where acoustic changes are known to be less extreme."
- This is a **single paper** (Interspeech-style, arXiv preprint), n≈19-20 listeners, one speaker, English only.

The supporting-evidence search surfaced a related ScienceDirect finding (S0167639315000977) that aligns: "at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast" — corroborating, not contradicting. No credible source contradicts the core finding; the general clear-speech-advantage literature concerns normal speeds, which is not what the claim addresses.

**Verdict**: The claim is accurately supported by a primary peer-reviewed-track source with a real human listening test and concrete numbers. The claim's framing ("challenging the assumption that clear-speech corpora are the best training target") is a reasonable inference. It is NOT refuted. The caveats (compression-artifact confound, read-vs-spontaneous clear speech, single study) reduce confidence to medium but do not overturn the claim.

input.evidence: PRIMARY SOURCE VERIFIED. Extracted the actual PDF of arXiv:1901.07239 ("Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates", Interspeech-track). The supporting quote is VERBATIM-ACCURATE — appears in both abstract (lines 23-24) and conclusions (lines 437-438): "even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech." This is a REAL HUMAN LISTENING TEST: "twenty native English speakers with no self reported hearing impairment transcribed eight sentences for each condition after hearing each sentence only once" (two experiments). Concrete word-error numbers at 3x rate: linearly compressed CLEAR speech (C-L) = 39.4% WER vs linearly compressed NORMAL (N-L) = 20.3% — clear speech ~2x the error. With duration equalized (compressing silence differently per style): N-NL1 = 15.3% vs C-NL2 = 28.2%, normal still better. The claim is directly and accurately supported. CAVEATS that lower confidence but do NOT refute: (1) The authors themselves flag a CONFOUND — clear speech "had to be compressed considerably more than normal speech which could have caused more artefacts" because the WSOLA implementation recommends <=4.0x and "for many sentences clear speech was compressed more than this"; so part of the normal advantage may be a compression-artifact effect, not an inherent style property ("Future work will involve reducing such artefacts"). (2) The clear speech tested was READ clear speech, which the authors note may be less robust than spontaneous/communicative clear speech "where acoustic changes are known to be less extreme." (3) Single study, n~19-20 listeners, one speaker, English only. WebSearch found NO contradicting source; a related ScienceDirect paper (S0167639315000977) corroborates ("at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast"). General clear-speech-advantage literature concerns normal speed, which the claim does not contest. The claim's inferential framing ("challenging the assumption that clear-speech corpora are the best training target") is a reasonable extrapolation flagged as such.
input.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Related corroborating source: ScienceDirect "Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style" (S0167639315000977) — aligns that clear-speech advantage reverses at highest rates. The only weakening factor is internal to the primary paper itself: a self-reported WSOLA over-compression artifact confound (>4.0x recommended limit) for clear speech, plus use of read (not spontaneous) clear speech, and single-study/small-n scope.

content.text: Verification complete. The claim is NOT refuted — it is verbatim-accurate to a primary source (arXiv:1901.07239) backed by a real human listening test with concrete word-error numbers (clear speech ~2x the error rate of normal at 3x compression, holding even after duration equalization). Confidence is medium rather than high because the paper itself flags a compression-artifact confound (clear speech exceeded the WSOLA 4.0x limit), used read rather than spontaneous clear speech, and is a single small-n study.
